description: Explains how pytest turns plain asserts into useful failure reports, how warning capture and warning assertions work, and how skip, fail, xfail, exit, raises, and import-based outcomes are represented.
document:
pytest keeps ordinary Python testing syntax at the center of its assertion design. The how-to guide shows users writing plain assert statements, while the import-time machinery in `AssertionRewritingHook` makes those assertions report the values of calls, attributes, comparisons, and operators when they fail. The hook only rewrites files pytest considers relevant, such as test modules, conftest files, and modules explicitly registered for assertion rewriting, then executes rewritten code and may cache it as a pytest-specific bytecode file. This lets users avoid a special assertion API while still seeing detailed failure explanations.

The assertion explanation layer is deliberately context-sensitive. `assertrepr_compare` supplies specialized descriptions for common comparisons, including text diffs, sequence index differences, set membership differences, dictionary key and value differences, approximate comparisons, and field-level comparisons for simple structured objects. If a project implements the comparison hook, pytest can replace the built-in explanation with the hook result. The formatting utility turns a compact internal explanation language into readable indented lines, and the truncation helper keeps very long assertion output short unless assertion verbosity is high, the run is on CI, or truncation is disabled by configuration.

Exception assertions are handled by `pytest.raises`, which can be used as a context manager or in the older function-call form. In the context-manager form it captures the exception in an ExceptionInfo object so tests can inspect its type, value, and traceback. Matching is by subclass, so an exact type requirement must be asserted separately. The helper can also check the formatted exception message with a regular expression, including notes attached to the exception, and a callable check can run after the type and message checks. For exception groups, the documentation points users toward the stricter group-aware helpers rather than relying on a containment check that cannot prove no unexpected exceptions were present.

Warnings are both reported by the test runner and assertable inside tests. The warnings plugin wraps configuration, collection, runtest, and summary phases in `catch_warnings_for_item`, applies ini, command-line, and per-item warning filters, and reports captured warnings through the recorded-warning hook. By default pytest makes deprecation warnings visible unless the interpreter already configured warning behavior. Inside a test, `pytest.warns` requires at least one warning of the expected category and can also apply a regex match to the warning message. Its recorder stores all emitted warnings, and when the context exits it re-emits warnings that did not match the expectation so nested warning checks do not silently swallow unrelated warnings. The recwarn fixture exposes the same recording interface across a whole test function, and `pytest.deprecated_call` is a convenience wrapper for the common deprecation categories.

Outcomes are represented as explicit exceptions so pytest can stop or classify execution without confusing those signals with ordinary assertion failures. The outcome module defines the base pytest outcome exception and the public helpers for fail, skip, xfail, and exit. Calling the skip helper during setup, call, teardown, doctests, or collection raises a skip outcome; a module-level skip is only allowed when the caller opts into that behavior. The import-or-skip helper imports a module or turns absence into a skip, and can also enforce a minimum version. Expected failures can be declared with a marker or raised imperatively; the imperative call stops the test immediately, while the marker still lets the test body run and can report an unexpected pass. Exit is separate because it terminates the test process with an optional return code rather than recording a normal test result.
